segments.text: This tiny bottle has the same shape
since 1968. And it's not because they're
lazy. It was designed by a Japanese
legend, Isamu Kenmoi. He designed some
clever features which is a huge
contributor for the success of Yakult.
First, the grip. Regular state bottles
are slippery, especially for kids. So,
Kenochi copied the shape of a
traditional Japanese keshi doll. It
creates a waist that fits perfectly
between a child's thumb and a finger,
and it never slips. But then comes the
flow problem. You know that tiny hole on
a coffee cup lid? Without it, the coffee
gets stuck and splashes. This waste does
the exact same thing. It creates a gap
for the air to enter so that the drink
flows perfectly smooth. But the biggest
question is why so tiny? Just 65 ml.
See, if you tore a big bottle, you would
drink half and save the rest. That's
dangerous. The good bacteria will die
when opened. So, they force the size to
be one shot. You finish it instantly,
getting the full dose of fresh bacteria
with zero waste. Subscribe for more
amazing design secrets.
